Multiple references confirm that the anterior two-thirds indeed differ embryologically due to originating primarily from the first pharyngeal arch. Yet importantly, newer insights emphasize the sulcus terminalisa distinctive feature arising directly from differences noted in embryonic development patterns between the first arch versus subsequent ones involved in forming the posterio-medial part of the tongue. Hence, although Passavants ridge plays a crucial role structurally, modern knowledge points strongly towards **Sulcus Terminalis**, marking exactly this transition point formed by fused lingual swellings coming from differing origins.
